title: Stability Was the Anomaly — Chaos Is the Baseline Now

source.original: 'Nothing Ever Happens' Is Over: The Fully Interconnected Startup — Naval Podcast

body_html:
For decades, a prevailing sense of geopolitical and technological calm lulled institutions and individuals into believing the world had reached a kind of equilibrium. Naval Ravikant's framing shatters that illusion: the period where nothing ever happens wasn't the norm — it was the exception. What we're entering now is a reversion to a more volatile mean, supercharged by exponential technologies that compress timelines for both progress and catastrophe.

This isn't merely about one crisis or one technology. It's the convergence of multiple accelerating vectors — AI, biotech, drone warfare, decentralized coordination — all maturing simultaneously. Each alone would be destabilizing; together they create a compounding effect where disruptions in one domain cascade unpredictably into others. The old playbook of incremental adjustment and institutional response is too slow for this tempo.

The ancient phrase may you live in interesting times was traditionally understood as a curse. Now it reads as simple description. The interesting times are here, and they're not a temporary aberration waiting to settle back into calm. The structural forces driving volatility — cheaper tools, faster information flow, lower barriers to both creation and destruction — are only intensifying.

What makes this moment uniquely high-stakes is that the same interconnectedness enabling a founder to build a global company from a laptop also enables a lone actor to access capabilities once reserved for nation-states. The system that produces extraordinary opportunity and extraordinary risk is the same system. You can't have one without the other.

For anyone making strategic decisions — whether in policy, investing, or building — the core reframe is clear: stop planning for stability and start building for resilience. The tail risks aren't tail risks anymore. They're becoming the main distribution.
